references.notes: The term 'الدَهر' (Dahr) refers to Time or Fate, which was often personified as a powerful, indifferent cosmological force in pre-Islamic Arabian belief.
The mention of 'غَول' (ghoul) explicitly refers to a type of monstrous supernatural being from pre-Islamic Arabian folklore.
This verse contains an oath formula swearing by the father, a common pre-Islamic practice where lineage and family honor served as a sacred anchor for oaths.
This verse features the oath formula 'By my life,' a widespread pre-Islamic expression used to affirm truth or commitment.
The verse explicitly mentions dwelling 'beneath the graves' (جاوَرتُ مِن تَحتِ القُبورِ), referring to the state of the deceased and thus touching upon pre-Islamic mortuary beliefs.
This verse contains an explicit oath by 'Allah,' who was recognized as a supreme deity in pre-Islamic Arabian belief.
The phrase refers to the practice of divination using marked arrows, a common pre-Islamic method for seeking guidance or fortune.
This verse contains an explicit oath 'By God,' which is a common pre-Islamic oath formula used to lend solemnity to a statement.
The personification of Dahr (Time/Fate) as an active, destructive force is a key pre-Islamic cosmological concept.
This phrase explicitly refers to the belief in jinn causing madness or possession.
Manāyā represents the pre-Islamic concept of fate or death as an irresistible, impersonal force.
This verse reiterates the concept of Manāyā as an inescapable force of death, fitting cosmological beliefs.
The term 'qada'' here signifies an inescapable decree of death, aligning with pre-Islamic notions of fate.
This refers to the grave or tomb, indicating a physical aspect of mortuary practice.
The term 'ḥatm' denotes an inevitable, predetermined fate, consistent with pre-Islamic cosmological views.
The verse contains an oath formula 'لَعَمرُ' sworn by 'أبي الطير' (father of the birds), which functions as a sacred entity in a pre-Islamic oath.
The verse includes an oath 'وَرَبّي' (by my Lord), which is a common pre-Islamic oath invoking a divine entity.
The term 'المَنِيَّةُ' (fate/death) is used here as an active force, reflecting the pre-Islamic cosmological concept of *manāyā* as an irresistible power.
The verse contains an oath 'وَأَبي' (by my father), which was a common and significant oath in pre-Islamic Arabian culture, invoking the reverence for one's ancestors.
The term 'al-Dahr' (Time/Fate) is invoked as a cosmological force, a common pre-Islamic concept of an impersonal agent of destiny.
This verse refers to the concept of blood vengeance (tha'r) and the reciprocal obligation to kill for a slain person, which was a quasi-ritual duty in pre-Islamic society.
The phrase 'yad al-Dahr' (the hand of Dahr) personifies Dahr as a controlling cosmological force, consistent with pre-Islamic fatalistic beliefs.
This verse contains an oath by 'Allāh,' which was a common pre-Islamic practice invoking a high god, fitting the category of oath formulae anchored to a sacred entity.

This verse contains an oath formula, 'لست لمرة' (I am not of Murra), which is a common pre-Islamic oath structure implying a profound disavowal of tribal affiliation if a condition is not met, thus anchoring it to a sacred social entity.
The phrase 'زُلَمٌ مِنَ القِداحِ' explicitly refers to divination arrows, a well-known practice of augury in pre-Islamic Arabia.
This verse contains an explicit oath invoking 'Allah', a common practice in pre-Islamic Arabian religion where Allah was one of the deities in the pantheon.
This verse contains an explicit oath invoking 'Allah', reflecting the pre-Islamic practice of swearing by deities.
The term 'al-ḥadathān' refers to the vicissitudes of time and fate, a key pre-Islamic cosmological concept of an impersonal, often destructive, force.
The term 'al-ḥutūf' (deaths/fates) signifies the impersonal forces of destiny and death, a common pre-Islamic cosmological idea.
The term 'ḥatf' (death/fate) refers to the concept of an inevitable, impersonal destiny, aligning with pre-Islamic cosmological beliefs.
The phrase 'ḥutūf al-dahr' explicitly refers to the fates brought by 'dahr' (time/fate), a central pre-Islamic cosmological concept of an active, often destructive, force.

The phrase 'اِستُحِلَّ حَرامُهُ' refers to the violation of something sacred or inviolable, a concept central to pre-Islamic notions of sacred boundaries and protection, often associated with sacred spaces or pacts.
The mention of an 'old bird' (طائر كهل) turning someone back is a clear reference to augury or divination by birds (ṭīra), a common pre-Islamic practice for interpreting omens.

The slaughtering of a young camel (naḥr al-bakr) as a condition for peace implies a ritualistic act, possibly a sacrifice or solemn offering to seal an agreement, which was a common pre-Islamic practice.

The phrase 'by the covenant of God' (bi-ʿahd Allāh) refers to a solemn oath or agreement made under divine witness, a practice common in pre-Islamic Arabian society where 'Allāh' was recognized as a supreme deity.
The term 'al-manāyā' (fates/deaths) refers to an impersonal, irresistible force of fate or death, a common pre-Islamic cosmological concept.
